title: Chocolate and hazelnut biscotti
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian biscuits for coffee
excerpt: Italian biscotti baked twice for a hard, dry crunch made for dipping into coffee or vin santo. Dark chocolate and toasted hazelnuts are folded through a plain dough and baked first as a log, then sliced and dried out in the oven. Makes about thirty.
sourceNotes: Biscotti di Prato, the original cantucci, are documented in Florentine and Tuscan household records from the late medieval period. The tradition of twice-baking biscuits to preserve them is recorded across Italian household manuals and the writings of Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891). The chocolate and hazelnut combination follows the northern Italian tradition documented in Turin and Piedmont, where gianduja (hazelnut-chocolate) is the regional flavour. No butter or fat in the dough is the defining characteristic of the Prato form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully cooled. Biscotti keep for 6 weeks in a tin; freezing is only necessary for longer storage.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Shape two logs and bake side by side.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Biscotti improve after 24 hours as they harden and the flavour develops. Bake a day ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 180°C in a conventional oven. Second bake at 150°C fan

glossaryTerms.definition: Literally 'twice-cooked' in Italian (bis = twice, cotti = cooked). The dough is first baked as a log until firm, then sliced while still warm and returned to the oven to dry out completely. The absence of fat means the result is hard enough to keep for several weeks in a tin and hard enough to require dipping in coffee or wine to soften before eating.

content.text: contain no butter or oil. The hard, dry texture is the point: it survives weeks in a tin and softens to something close to cake when dipped in coffee. The chocolate here is folded in as chunks rather than cocoa powder, so the pieces melt against the dry crumb. Toast the hazelnuts first.

Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} with the {{vanilla-extract}} and pour into the dry ingredients. Mix until a stiff, sticky dough forms. Fold in the {{dark-chocolate}} and {{hazelnuts}}.

With wet hands, shape the dough into a log about 28 cm long and 8 cm wide on a lined baking tray. Bake for 25–30 minutes, until the surface feels firm and set when pressed. Remove from the oven and rest for 15 minutes.

Lower the oven to 150°C fan. Using a sharp serrated knife, cut the log into slices 1.5 cm thick on a slight diagonal. Lay them flat on the tray and bake for 12–15 minutes per side, until the cut surfaces are dry and no longer give when pressed. Cool on a rack. They will harden further as they cool.
